name: Rhodiola
summary: Rhodiola may support stress-related fatigue and mental stamina; avoid framing it as a bedtime calming herb.
description: Adaptogen-style fatigue support with activation/insomnia caution in sensitive users.
safetyNotes: Generally well tolerated for most users.

claimMap.claim: Rhodiola may help mild depression/anxiety/mood outcomes, but evidence is not definitive and better RCTs are needed.
claimMap.notes: Rhodiola may help mild depression/anxiety/mood outcomes, but evidence is not definitive and better RCTs are needed.

claimMap.claim: Rhodiola may reduce some symptoms of stress-related or mental fatigue in selected populations, but the clinical literature is heterogeneous and frequently at risk of bias; the effect should be framed as preliminary rather than established.
claimMap.notes: Systematic reviews identified signals for fatigue outcomes, but no included study in the 2012 review was free of plausible bias, and a later rigorously conducted nursing-student trial was inconclusive.

claimMap.claim: Pure salidroside at 60 mg/day for 16 days has exploratory evidence in active young adults, but it is not interchangeable with whole Rhodiola extract.
claimMap.notes: Pure salidroside at 60 mg/day for 16 days has exploratory evidence in active young adults, but it is not interchangeable with whole Rhodiola extract.

qualifiers.dose_or_duration: Pure biosynthetic salidroside 60 mg/day for 16 days

claimMap.claim: Higher short-term Rhodiola doses around 1500–2400 mg/day for 4–30 days have been studied mainly for exercise-performance contexts.
claimMap.notes: Higher short-term Rhodiola doses around 1500–2400 mg/day for 4–30 days have been studied mainly for exercise-performance contexts.

qualifiers.dose_or_duration: Recent trials used ~1500–2400 mg Rhodiola rosea/day for 4–30 days
qualifiers.population: Trained and untrained adults in recent trials

claimMap.claim: Rhodiola has encouraging clinical evidence for stress-related fatigue and mental performance, but extract standardization and study design vary.
claimMap.notes: Rhodiola has encouraging clinical evidence for stress-related fatigue and mental performance, but extract standardization and study design vary.

qualifiers.dose_or_duration: Rhodiola rosea preparations; extract standardization varies

claimMap.claim: Rhodiola exercise-performance studies report mixed results across acute and short-term protocols; differences in participants, doses, outcomes, and extract composition prevent a reliable broad ergogenic claim.
claimMap.notes: Recent systematic reviews describe variable findings and substantial protocol and product heterogeneity; this claim intentionally avoids promising a general endurance or recovery benefit.

claimMap.claim: Rhodiola pages should include potential drug-interaction/contraindication warnings and avoid disease-treatment claims.
claimMap.notes: Rhodiola pages should include potential drug-interaction/contraindication warnings and avoid disease-treatment claims.

qualifiers.direction: caution for interactions and specific populations

claimMap.claim: Rhodiola appears tolerated in short exercise/sport studies, but safety claims should stay modest because the evidence base is small.
claimMap.notes: Rhodiola appears tolerated in short exercise/sport studies, but safety claims should stay modest because the evidence base is small.

qualifiers.direction: no obvious adverse reactions reported; evidence limited

claimMap.claim: Rhodiola evidence and safety pages should disclose that RCT reporting quality has been generally poor, limiting confidence in safety and efficacy conclusions.
claimMap.notes: Rhodiola evidence and safety pages should disclose that RCT reporting quality has been generally poor, limiting confidence in safety and efficacy conclusions.

qualifiers.direction: poor reporting / unclear risk of bias

claimMap.claim: For exercise-related fatigue/performance, some acute studies use ~200 mg standardized Rhodiola rosea about 60 minutes before exercise.
claimMap.notes: For exercise-related fatigue/performance, some acute studies use ~200 mg standardized Rhodiola rosea about 60 minutes before exercise.

qualifiers.dose_or_duration: Acute ~200 mg Rhodiola rosea containing ~1% salidroside and ~3% rosavin, about 60 minutes before exercise
qualifiers.population: Recreationally active males and females in reviewed acute studies
